serving.natural_language_portion: A standard side-dish serving of Bhindi Masala is approximately 150 g, equivalent to a medium bowl alongside roti or paratha at a typical Indian meal.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods
nutrient_source.organization: National Institute of Nutrition (NIN), ICMR, India
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values for Bhindi Masala are adapted from ICMR-NIN 'Nutritive Value of Indian Foods' using cooked okra and spiced vegetable preparation data. Values for cholesterol, saturated fat, and trans fat were cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (okra, cooked) to confirm plausibility. The composite dish values reflect typical home-style preparation with moderate oil.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index and fiber content of Abelmoschus esculentus (okra)
gi_source.organization: Journal of Food Science and Technology (Springer)
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI value of 25 is sourced from peer-reviewed research on Abelmoschus esculentus (okra) published in the Journal of Food Science and Technology. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (25 × 15.0 g) / 100 = 3.75, rounded to 3.8. Available carbohydrate per 150 g serving = 10.0 g × 1.5 = 15.0 g. The low GI is attributed to okra's high soluble fibre (mucilage) content which slows glucose absorption.

hypertension.key_factor: Moderate sodium content (260 mg/100 g) offset by high potassium (285 mg/100 g); favourable K:Na ratio supports blood pressure management
hypertension.guidance: Bhindi Masala is suitable for hypertensive individuals when prepared with minimal added salt; its naturally high potassium content from okra and tomatoes supports vasodilation and blood pressure regulation, but sodium should be monitored in larger servings.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic index (GI 25) and high dietary fibre (3.2 g/100 g) from okra mucilage significantly attenuate postprandial glucose response
type2_diabetes.guidance: Bhindi Masala is an excellent choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; the low GI of 25 and high soluble fibre content slow glucose absorption, and a standard 150 g serving delivers a glycemic load of only 3.8, making it compatible with blood sugar management strategies.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (1.2 g/100 g), zero cholesterol, and zero trans fat; okra provides antioxidant polyphenols and soluble fibre that support lipid profile improvement
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Bhindi Masala presents a heart-healthy nutritional profile with negligible saturated fat and no cholesterol; the soluble fibre in okra has been associated with LDL cholesterol reduction, and the dish's antioxidant spices (turmeric, ginger) provide additional cardioprotective benefit when prepared with minimal oil.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory spices (turmeric, ginger) and antioxidant vitamins C and K present in the dish
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Bhindi Masala contains turmeric and ginger, both of which have documented anti-inflammatory properties, and provides vitamins C and K that support joint health; it is generally well-tolerated by individuals with rheumatoid arthritis.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Bhindi Masala is a classic North Indian vegetarian side dish made from okra (lady's finger, Abelmoschus esculentus) stir-fried until tender and slightly crisp, then combined with a spiced onion-tomato masala base. The cooking technique involves sautéing the okra separately at high heat to minimise its characteristic sliminess before folding it into a sauce of sautéed onions, tomatoes, ginger-garlic paste, and aromatic spices including turmeric, coriander powder, cumin, red chilli, and amchoor (dried mango powder). Amchoor imparts a characteristic tangy sourness that balances the earthy, slightly grassy flavour of okra. The finished dish is dry to semi-dry in texture, with tender okra pieces coated in a fragrant, slightly caramelised masala. It is typically served as a side dish alongside roti, paratha, or dal-rice combinations in everyday North Indian meals. Nutritionally, Bhindi Masala is a low-calorie, high-fibre preparation (128 kcal and 3.2 g dietary fibre per 100 g) with a very low glycemic index of 25, making it particularly suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or seeking blood sugar control. The dish is entirely plant-based (vegan), gluten-free, and contains no cholesterol. Its potassium-to-sodium ratio is favourable for cardiovascular and hypertension management. Okra's soluble fibre (mucilage) has been associated with LDL cholesterol reduction and improved glycaemic control in clinical literature.
common_user_queries: Is bhindi masala good for diabetes?
How many calories in bhindi masala?
What spices are in bhindi masala?
Can I eat bhindi masala if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN 'Nutritive Value of Indian Foods', which is an approved primary source for Indian dishes. However, Bhindi Masala as a composite prepared dish does not have a single exact entry; values are derived from cooked okra and spiced vegetable preparation data with reasonable adaptation. GI is supported by a peer-reviewed journal study on okra. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. trans_fat_g is set to 0.0: confirmed negligible/zero for a home-style vegetable preparation using standard cooking oils per ICMR-NIN and USDA FoodData Central data. cholesterol_mg is set to 0.0: confirmed zero as this is a fully plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients. vitamin_b12_mcg is set to 0.0: confirmed zero as this is a vegan dish; vitamin B12 is not present in plant foods. vitamin_d_mcg is set to 0.0: confirmed negligible for this plant-based preparation with no fortified ingredients. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite Indian vegetable preparation is not individually reported in ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods or USDA FoodData Central for this specific dish. water_g is set to -1: moisture content for this composite prepared dish is not reported in the primary source consulted. 3. Portions: the input contained duplicate portion entries; the canonical serving size of 150 g (standard side-dish serving) has been adopted as the default, consistent with ICMR-NIN typical serving guidance for Indian vegetable side dishes. 4. GI source: the Journal of Food Science and Technology (Springer) is a peer-reviewed journal and qualifies as an approved GI source under Project Veritas v2 source policy. 5. Multilingual coverage: this is a North Indian dish; Hindi local name is mandatory and present. Tamil alias (Vendakkai Masala) added for South Indian regional recognition. Bengali and Punjabi aliases preserved from input.
